items.myth: If training runs without NaNs, the learning rate must be fine.
items.reality: Stable but tiny η can produce glacial progress that wastes epochs; always judge η by loss/ metric improvement rate, not absence of errors.
items.example: η=1e-7 on fine-tuning may never NaN yet validation accuracy stays within 0.1% of the pretrained baseline after 10 epochs.

items.myth: Adaptive optimizers like Adam eliminate the need to choose a learning rate.
items.reality: Adam uses a base learning rate that scales all updates; it adapts relative step sizes per parameter but η remains a critical hyperparameter.
items.example: Same Adam setup with lr=1e-2 vs lr=1e-4 can mean convergence in hours vs failure to learn on the same task.

items.myth: A larger batch size always allows a proportionally larger learning rate.
items.reality: Linear scaling rules (double batch, double η) are heuristics that sometimes help but are not guaranteed; empirical validation still matters.
items.example: Doubling batch 32→64 and η 0.1→0.2 may diverge on a noisy dataset even though the rule-of-thumb suggests it should work.
